papers.title: Hypervelocity Stars in the Gaia era. Revisiting the most extreme stars from the MMT survey
papers.author_names: S. Kreuzer A. Irrgang U. Heber

abstract.text: The hypervelocity star (HVS) survey conducted at the Multiple Mirror Telescope (MMT) identified 42 B-type stars in the Galactic halo whose radial velocity in the Galactic rest-frame exceeds $+275\\\\,$km$\\\\,$s${}^{-1}$. In order to unravel the nature and origin of those high-velocity outliers, their complete six-dimensional phase space information is needed. To this end, we complemented positions and proper motions from the second data release of {\\\\it Gaia} with revised radial velocities and spectrophotometric distances that are based on a reanalysis of the available MMT spectra of 40 objects using state-of-the-art model spectra and a tailored analysis strategy. The resulting position and velocity vectors for 37 stars were then used as input for a subsequent kinematic investigation to obtain as complete a picture as possible. The combination of projected rotational velocity, position in the Kiel diagram, and kinematic properties suggests that all objects in the sample except two (B576, B598) are very likely to be main sequence stars. While the available data are still not precise enough to constrain the place of origin for 19 program stars, we identified eight objects that either come from the outer rim of the Galactic disk or not from the disk at all, along with ten that presumably stem from the Galactic disk. For almost all of those 18 targets with more or less well-constrained spatial origin, the Galactic center (GC) is disqualified as a possible place of origin. The most notable exception is B576, the origin of which coincides extremely well with the GC when assuming a blue horizontal branch (BHB) nature for it. HVS$\\\\,$22 is by far the most extreme object in the sample. Although its origin is completely unconstrained, an ejection from the GC by the Hills mechanism is the most plausible explanation for its current Galactic rest-frame velocity of $1530^{+690}_{-560}\\\\,$km$\\\\,$s${}^{-1}$.

brief.tldr: [research paper] A reanalysis of 40 MMT HVS survey objects using updated model spectra and kinematic modeling reveals that 37 of the 42 B-type hypervelocity stars are likely main sequence stars, with most originating from the Galactic disk or outer rim rather than the Galactic center. Eight stars are traced to the outer disk or non-disk regions, while ten originate within the disk; the Galactic center is ruled out for 18 objects, except B576, which may have originated there as a blue horizontal branch star. HVS 22, with a velocity of 1530 km/s, is the most extreme and is best explained by a Hills mechanism ejection from the Galactic center despite lack of spatial origin constraints.

source_fields.title: Hypervelocity Stars in the Gaia era. Revisiting the most extreme stars from the MMT survey

source_fields.sections_text: Results: The experiment finds that 19 stars have unconstrained origins due to large kinematic uncertainties, with most ejection velocities and flight times suggesting a Galactic disk origin, and all stars except B598 are consistent with an ejection scenario from the Galactic plane within their inferred main-sequence lifetimes, as evidenced by flight times being less than or comparable to their evolutionary ages. Conclusion: This study identifies the origins and kinematic properties of 40 hypervelocity stars, finding that most originate in the Galactic disk with some from the outer rim, while a few—including B576 and HVS 22—likely resulted from Hills' slingshot mechanism near the Galactic center or in extreme ejection events. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel dynamical mechanism—stellar ejection via gravitational interactions in dense environments—that can explain the observed hypervelocity stars with speeds exceeding 500 km s⁻¹, addressing the limitation of classical binary supernova and dynamical ejection scenarios in producing such extreme velocities. Spectral analysis: This section presents a refined spectral analysis method using flux-calibrated MMT spectra to improve the accuracy of stellar temperature and gravity determinations by leveraging continuum shape and Balmer jump features, validated against higher-resolution spectra. Kinematic analysis: The kinematic analysis uses six-dimensional phase space data from Gaia and HST to trace the trajectories of 37 runaway stars back in time, accounting for uncertainties and Galactic rotation to determine their spatial origins and ejection velocities. SEDs and spectrophotometric distances: Spectrophotometric distances are derived by fitting observed spectral energy distributions (SEDs) with synthetic ones to determine angular diameters, interstellar reddening, and surface gravity, then combining these with evolutionary models to estimate stellar distances. Model atmospheres and synthetic spectra: The paper proposes an improved ADS approach that iteratively combines ATLAS12, DETAIL, and SURFACE to compute realistic synthetic spectra for B-type stars, incorporating non-LTE effects and advanced line broadening to accurately model the Balmer jump region.

The resulting position and velocity vectors for 37 stars were then used as input for a subsequent kinematic investigation to obtain as complete a picture as possible.

papers.title: How runaway stars boost galactic outflows
papers.author_names: Eric P. Andersson Oscar Agertz Florent Renaud

abstract.text: Roughly ten per cent of OB stars are kicked out of their natal clusters before ending their life as supernovae. These so-called runaway stars can travel hundreds of parsecs into the low-density interstellar medium, where momentum and energy from stellar feedback is efficiently deposited. In this work we explore how this mechanism affects large scale properties of the galaxy, such as outflows. To do so we use a new model which treats OB stars and their associated feedback processes on a star-by-star basis. With this model we compare two hydrodynamical simulations of Milky Way-like galaxies, one where we include runaways, and one where we ignore them. Including runaway stars leads to twice as many supernovae explosions in regions with gas densities ranging from 1e-5 cm^-3 to 1e-3 cm^-3. This results in more efficient heating of the inter-arm regions, and drives strong galactic winds with mass loading factors boosted by up to one order of magnitude. These outflows produce a more massive and extended multi-phase circumgalactic medium, as well as a population of dense clouds in the halo. Conversely, since less energy and momentum is released in the dense star forming regions, the cold phase of the interstellar medium is less disturbed by feedback effects.

brief.tldr: [research paper] A star-by-star hydrodynamical model incorporating runaway OB stars—kicked out of their natal clusters due to dynamical interactions—reveals that their supernova feedback significantly enhances galactic outflows. In low-density interstellar regions (10⁻⁵–10⁻³ cm⁻³), runaways trigger twice as many supernovae, leading to up to one order of magnitude higher mass loading in galactic winds, a more massive and extended circumgalactic medium, and dense halo clouds. The absence of such feedback in dense star-forming regions reduces cold ISM disturbance, highlighting a key role of runaways in regulating baryon cycling and outflow efficiency.

source_fields.title: How runaway stars boost galactic outflows

source_fields.venue: Monthly notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Results: Including runaway stars suppresses early star formation by ~120 Myr, leading to more structured, hotter gaseous halos with large $\\sim1$ kpc bubbles and prominent outflows, while only subtly altering stellar luminosity and maintaining stable SFRs afterward. Discussion: Simulations show that runaway stars increase galactic outflow mass loading by enhancing energy deposition in the halo and reducing cold ISM mass, leading to more efficient star formation regulation over cosmic time with a higher mass loading and thermal energy input compared to models without runaway stars. Conclusions: Including runaway stars in hydrodynamical simulations significantly enhances stellar feedback efficiency, drives stronger galactic outflows, increases hot gas mass in the halo, and alters feedback distribution without disrupting the cold ISM—highlighting the need to model feedback source relocation rather than simply increasing feedback strength. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel framework that explicitly models the coupling between individual star formation and stellar feedback at subgrid scales, addressing the persistent challenge of accurately capturing the physics of gas cooling, angular momentum loss, and outflow dynamics in high-resolution galaxy simulations. Numerical setup: This section describes the numerical setup of two simulations of an isolated star-forming galaxy using the RAMSES code with AMR, incorporating runaway star dynamics, realistic initial conditions based on the AGORA project, and physical processes including star formation and metallicity-dependent cooling. Star formation and stellar feedback: This section describes a computational method for sampling massive stars from a Kroupa IMF, treating stellar feedback from winds and supernovae, and chemically enriching the ISM, while ensuring mass conservation and efficient simulation through bin-based Poisson sampling.

abstract: Roughly ten per cent of OB stars are kicked out of their natal clusters before ending their life as supernovae.
These so-called runaway stars can travel hundreds of parsecs into the low-density interstellar medium, where momentum and energy from stellar feedback is efficiently deposited.
To do so we use a new model which treats OB stars and their associated feedback processes on a star-by-star basis.
Including runaway stars leads to twice as many supernovae explosions in regions with gas densities ranging from 1e-5 cm^-3 to 1e-3 cm^-3.
title: How runaway stars boost galactic outflows

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical/simulation paper exploring effects of runaway stars on galactic outflows using hydrodynamical models, no observational stellar data presented.

papers.title: A kinematically hot population of young stars in the solar neighbourhood
papers.author_names: A. S. Binks R. D. Jeffries N. J. Wright

abstract.text: In the last three decades several hundred nearby members of young stellar moving groups (MGs) have been identified, but there has been less systematic effort to quantify or characterise young stars that do not belong to previously identified MGs. Using a kinematically unbiased sample of 225 lithium-rich stars within 100 pc, we find that only $50 \\\\pm 10$ per cent of young ($\\\\lesssim 125$ Myr), low-mass ($0.5<M/M_{\\\\odot}<1.0$) stars, are kinematically associated with known MGs. Whilst we find some evidence that six of the non-MG stars may be connected with the Lower Centaurus-Crux association, the rest form a kinematically "hotter" population, much more broadly dispersed in velocity, and with no obvious concentrations in space. The mass distributions of the MG members and non-MG stars is similar, but the non-MG stars may be older on average. We briefly discuss several explanations for the origin of the non-MG population.

source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Summary: This study identifies a kinematically unbiased sample of young low-mass stars, finds that half are members of known moving groups, and suggests that the unassigned, "hot" stars may originate from unrecognized groups, low-density star formation, or ejection from clusters, requiring further age and phase-space analysis to resolve. Discussion: About half of young, Li-rich stars within 100 pc are kinematically associated with known moving groups, while the remainder—particularly those with higher velocity dispersions and velocities up to 40 km s⁻¹ offset from group members—suggest a significant population of non-members, including a newly identified kinematic substructure of five stars potentially belonging to an unrecognized young stellar stream. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel kinematic analysis of Li-rich, nearby young stars from Gaia DR2, addressing the problem of identifying young stars not associated with known moving groups by quantifying their 3D kinematics and revealing a significant fraction of previously unclassified young stars. Input Catalogue: The input catalogue comprises 2349 kinematically unbiased young stellar candidates selected from X-ray and spectroscopic surveys, filtered by GDR2 astrometric quality and Li abundance criteria, to identify nearby, young stars based on their spectral type and Li line strength relative to the Pleiades cluster. Assigning moving group membership: Assigning moving group membership requires balancing kinematic consistency with spatial information, but challenges arise due to potential spatial dispersion of members and uncertainties in the initial spatial proximity and representativeness of defining members. Kinematics of young, nearby stars: A significant fraction of young, nearby stars (89/225) lack kinematic membership in known nearby young groups, with non-member stars distributed broadly across the sky and showing an increasing proportion with age, particularly among low-mass stars. Appendix A Results from the BANYAN$\\Sigma$ analysis: The BANYAN$\\Sigma$ analysis identifies a kinematically hot population of young stars in the solar neighbourhood with >80% membership probability, showing that these stars are younger than the solar age and exhibit high velocity dispersions consistent with a dynamically young and turbulent origin.

papers.title: A high-resolution spectroscopic study of two new Na- and Al-rich field giants -- likely globular cluster escapees in the Galactic halo
papers.author_names: Avrajit Bandyopadhyay Sivarani Thirupathi Timothy C. Beers A.Susmitha

abstract.text: The stars SDSS J064655.6+411620.5 and SDSS J193712.01+502455.5 are relatively bright stars that were initially observed as part of the SDSS/MARVELS pre-survey. They were selected, on the basis of their weak CH $G$-bands, along with a total of 60 others, in the range of halo globular cluster metallicities for high-resolution spectroscopic follow-up as a part of the HESP-GOMPA survey (Hanle Echelle SPectrograph -- Galactic survey Of Metal Poor stArs). The stars exhibit typical nucleosynthesis signatures expected from the so-called second-generation stars of globular clusters. The light-element anti-correlation of Mg-Al is detected, along with elevated abundances of Na. Carbon is found to be depleted, which is compatible with expectation. Lithium is also detected in SDSS J064655.6+411620.5 and SDSS J193712.01+502455.5; the measured abundances are similar to those of normal halo giant stars. These bright escapees provide a unique opportunity to study the nucleosynthesis events of globular clusters in great detail, and shed light on their chemical-enrichment histories.

source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Abundances: This section reports the chemical abundances of light, α-elements, and Fe-peak elements in two giant stars, revealing enhanced oxygen and α-elements consistent with Galactic halo origins, with notable Na and Al detections that help trace Galactic chemical evolution. Conclusion: Two high-resolution spectroscopic observations of GC escapees with light-element anomalies suggest binary mass transfer or massive star wind pollution is unlikely, and future surveys combined with Gaia kinematics and asteroseismology will help determine their origin and frequency. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel framework that links the observed chemical anomalies (C, N, O, Na, Mg, Al) in globular cluster stars to self-pollution by hot-bottom burning in intermediate-to-high mass AGB stars and fast-rotating massive stars, providing a unified explanation for mass loss and chemical evolution in GCs due to internal nucleosynthesis processes rather than external tidal interactions. Acknowledgement: This section acknowledges the reviewers, observational facilities, and funding sources, while presenting detailed observational and elemental abundance data for two target stars. Results and Discussion: The two program stars exhibit GC-like [Na/Fe] and [Al/Fe] enhancements, under-abundances of [C/Fe] and [O/Fe], and depleted [Mg/Fe], with [$\\alpha$/Fe] = +0.22, consistent with Galactic halo orbits and second-generation GC characteristics, ruling out thick-disc membership. Observations and Analysis: High-resolution spectroscopic observations and detailed atmospheric parameter analysis of two metal-poor halo stars identified from MARVELS pre-survey data reveal their chemical and physical properties, with two candidates showing evidence of being Galactic halo escapees.
